1:
"And Joseph fell upon his father's face, and wept upon him, and kissed him." The witnesses note Joseph's grief over his dead father. On "Joseph fell upon his father's face, and wept upon him," the grammatical voices note the grief. Gill and Henry note that "Joseph fell upon his father's face, and wept upon him, and kissed him" — Joseph, at Jacob's death (49:33), fell on his father's face, weeping and kissing him; the deep grief of the loving son; the tender mourning; the affection of Joseph for the father he had so lately regained (46:29).

On "wept upon him," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the mourning. Calvin and Gill note Joseph's weeping (the natural, lawful grief, even of the godly, at the death of a beloved father); the tears of true affection (as Jesus wept, John 11:35); the mourning not forbidden, but the excess to be moderated by faith.

On "and kissed him," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the tenderness: "and kissed him" — Joseph's last kiss on the dead father (the tender farewell); the closing of Jacob's eyes by Joseph (as promised, 46:4); the love of the son; the honor to the father at his death (the godly grief). The verse displays Joseph's grief over his dead father.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the grief — "Joseph fell upon his father's face, and wept upon him, and kissed him," the deep mourning (49:33, Gill, Henry); "wept upon him," the lawful grief (John 11:35, Calvin, Gill); "and kissed him," the tender farewell (46:4, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the grieving Joseph. The verse marks the grief: deep. The verse displays the weeping: upon him. The verse displays the kiss: the farewell. The verse displays the love: of the son. The grief displays Joseph's mourning over his father. The verse displays the Joseph: weeping. The verse displays the father: dead. The verse displays the affection: tender. Joseph's grief over his dead father displays the godly mourning: Joseph fell on his father's face, wept, and kissed him (49:33; 46:4; John 11:35) — the deep, lawful grief of the loving son, the tender farewell. The verse displays the mourning Joseph, who commands the embalming (v.2).
2:
"And Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father: and the physicians embalmed Israel." The witnesses note Jacob embalmed after the Egyptian custom. On "Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father," the grammatical voices note the embalming. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father: and the physicians embalmed Israel" — Joseph had Jacob embalmed (the Egyptian art of preserving the dead, by which the body was kept from corruption); the physicians (the embalmers) doing the work; the Egyptian custom followed (for the long journey to Canaan, vv.7-13, and the honor of the patriarch).

On "his servants the physicians," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the custom. Calvin and Ellicott note the embalming (the Egyptian practice, well-known, the body preserved with spices and natron) — the custom of the land; the honor done to Jacob; the practical necessity (for the journey to the burial in Canaan).

On "the physicians embalmed Israel," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the honor: "the physicians embalmed Israel" — Jacob (Israel) embalmed (the honor of the patriarch, like the embalming of Joseph himself, v.26); the body preserved; the providence (the embalmed body to be carried to Canaan, vv.7-13, the faith that buried in the promised land). The verse displays Jacob embalmed after the Egyptian custom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the embalming — "Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father," the Egyptian art (Keil, Gill); "his servants the physicians," the custom (Calvin, Ellicott); "the physicians embalmed Israel," the honor (v.26, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the embalmed Jacob. The verse marks the embalming: commanded. The verse displays the physicians: the embalmers. The verse displays the custom: Egyptian. The verse displays the honor: to the patriarch. The embalming displays Jacob preserved for the journey. The verse displays the Joseph: commanding. The verse displays the Israel: embalmed. The verse displays the burial: to come. Jacob embalmed after the Egyptian custom displays the honor and the providence: Joseph had the physicians embalm Israel (v.26) — the Egyptian art preserving the patriarch for the journey to the burial in Canaan. The verse displays the embalmed Jacob, mourned the forty days (v.3).
3:
"And forty days were fulfilled for him; for so are fulfilled the days of those which are embalmed: and the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days." The very brief verse notes the mourning for Jacob. The grammatical voices note the days. Ellicott and Gill note that "forty days were fulfilled for him... and the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days" — the embalming took forty days (the Egyptian period); and the Egyptians mourned Jacob seventy days (the period of mourning, nearly that of a king, the honor done to Jacob for Joseph's sake); the great mourning; the honor of the patriarch.

On "forty days were fulfilled," the witnesses note the embalming. Gill notes the forty days of embalming (the Egyptian art, the time it took) — the preparing of the body; the custom.

On "the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the honor: "the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days" — the seventy days of mourning (the honor, nearly royal, done to Jacob for Joseph's sake); the great esteem (the whole land mourning the father of the governor); the honor to the patriarch (the providence exalting the covenant family even in death). The verse displays the mourning for Jacob.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the days — "forty days... and the Egyptians mourned for him threescore and ten days," the embalming and mourning (Ellicott, Gill); "forty days were fulfilled," the embalming (Gill); "the Egyptians mourned... threescore and ten days," the honor (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mourned Jacob. The verse marks the embalming: forty days. The verse displays the mourning: seventy days. The verse displays the honor: nearly royal. The verse displays the esteem: great. The mourning displays the honor done to Jacob. The verse displays the Jacob: mourned. The verse displays the Egyptians: mourning. The verse displays the honor: great. The mourning for Jacob displays the honor of the patriarch: forty days embalmed, seventy days mourned by the Egyptians — the nearly-royal honor done to Jacob for Joseph's sake. The verse displays the mourned Jacob, before the journey (vv.4-6).
4:
"And when the days of his mourning were past, Joseph spake unto the house of Pharaoh, saying, If now I have found grace in your eyes, speak, I pray you, in the ears of Pharaoh, saying," The very brief verse notes Joseph seeking leave to bury Jacob in Canaan. The grammatical voices note the request. Ellicott and Gill note that "Joseph spake unto the house of Pharaoh... If now I have found grace in your eyes, speak... in the ears of Pharaoh" — Joseph, the mourning past, asked the courtiers to intercede with Pharaoh for leave to go bury his father in Canaan; the courteous request (Joseph, though governor, seeking leave, perhaps because, as a mourner, he did not appear before Pharaoh directly, or out of deference); the seeking of permission.

On "If now I have found grace in your eyes," the witnesses note the courtesy. Gill notes Joseph's courteous, humble request (through the courtiers) — the deference; the seeking of leave (for the journey to Canaan, vv.5-6).

On "speak... in the ears of Pharaoh," the grammatical-historical note the intercession: "speak, I pray you, in the ears of Pharaoh" — Joseph asking the courtiers to bring his request before Pharaoh; the seeking of leave to bury Jacob in Canaan (the oath, v.5); the providence (the burial in the promised land). The verse displays Joseph seeking leave to bury Jacob in Canaan.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the request — "If now I have found grace in your eyes, speak... in the ears of Pharaoh," the seeking of leave (vv.5-6, Ellicott, Gill); "If now I have found grace in your eyes," the courtesy (Gill); "speak... in the ears of Pharaoh," the intercession (v.5, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the requesting Joseph. The verse marks the request: leave to bury. The verse displays the courtiers: to intercede. The verse displays the Pharaoh: to be asked. The verse displays the Canaan: the burial. The request displays Joseph seeking leave for the burial. The verse displays the Joseph: requesting. The verse displays the courtiers: asked. The verse displays the burial: in Canaan. Joseph seeking leave to bury Jacob in Canaan displays the courteous request: speak in Pharaoh's ears, that I may bury my father (v.5) — the deference of the governor, seeking leave for the burial in the promised land. The verse displays the requesting Joseph, citing the oath (v.5).
5:
"My father made me swear, saying, Lo, I die: in my grave which I have digged for me in the land of Canaan, there shalt thou bury me. Now therefore let me go up, I pray thee, and bury my father, and I will come again." The witnesses note Joseph citing Jacob's oath to bury him in Canaan. On "My father made me swear... in my grave which I have digged for me in the land of Canaan," the grammatical voices note the oath. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "My father made me swear, saying... in my grave which I have digged for me in the land of Canaan, there shalt thou bury me" — Joseph cited Jacob's oath (47:29-31): the dying father had made Joseph swear to bury him in Canaan (in the grave he had prepared, the cave of Machpelah, 49:29-31); the binding oath; the reason for the journey (the burial in the promised land).

On "which I have digged for me," the reformed and witnesses note the grave. Gill notes the grave (the cave of Machpelah, 49:30, bought by Abraham, 23:16-20, where the patriarchs were buried) — Jacob's chosen burial-place; the family sepulchre; the burial in the covenant land.

On "let me go up... and bury my father, and I will come again," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the request: "let me go up... and bury my father, and I will come again" — Joseph asking leave (citing the oath as his warrant), promising to return; the faith that buried in Canaan (not Egypt, the dying patriarch looking to the promised land, Hebrews 11:13-22); the providence (the burial in the land of promise). The verse displays Joseph citing Jacob's oath to bury him in Canaan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oath — "My father made me swear... there shalt thou bury me," the oath (47:29, Keil, Gill); "which I have digged for me," the grave (49:30; 23:16, Gill); "let me go up... and bury my father, and I will come again," the request (Hebrews 11:13, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the citing Joseph. The verse marks the oath: to bury in Canaan. The verse displays the grave: Machpelah. The verse displays the request: leave to go. The verse displays the faith: in the land. The citation displays Joseph's warrant for the journey. The verse displays the Joseph: citing. The verse displays the oath: binding. The verse displays the Canaan: the burial. Joseph citing Jacob's oath to bury him in Canaan displays the warrant and the faith: my father made me swear to bury him in Canaan (47:29; 49:30; Hebrews 11:13-22) — the binding oath, the faith that buried in the promised land. The verse displays the citing Joseph, granted leave (v.6).
6:
"And Pharaoh said, Go up, and bury thy father, according as he made thee swear." The very brief verse notes Pharaoh granting leave for the burial. The grammatical voices note the leave. Gill and Calvin note that "Pharaoh said, Go up, and bury thy father, according as he made thee swear" — Pharaoh granted Joseph leave to go bury Jacob in Canaan, in keeping with the oath; the king's ready permission (the honor to Joseph, and to Jacob); the leave given for the journey (vv.7-13).

On "Go up, and bury thy father," the witnesses note the permission. Gill notes Pharaoh's leave (the ready grant) — the honor to Joseph; the permission for the burial; the king's favor.

On "according as he made thee swear," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the oath honored: "according as he made thee swear" — Pharaoh honoring the oath (the binding vow); the leave to fulfill it; the providence (the burial in Canaan, the faith of the patriarchs honored even by the heathen king). The verse displays Pharaoh granting leave for the burial.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the leave — "Go up, and bury thy father, according as he made thee swear," the permission (Gill, Calvin); "Go up, and bury thy father," the leave (Gill); "according as he made thee swear," the oath honored (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the granting Pharaoh. The verse marks the leave: go up. The verse displays the burial: granted. The verse displays the oath: honored. The verse displays the favor: of the king. The leave displays Pharaoh permitting the burial. The verse displays the Pharaoh: granting. The verse displays the Joseph: free to go. The verse displays the oath: honored. Pharaoh granting leave for the burial displays the king's favor and the oath honored: go up, and bury thy father, as he made thee swear — the ready permission, the burial in Canaan honored even by the heathen king. The verse displays the journeying company (vv.7-9). That the king who had so honoured Joseph in life now readily granted him leave to bury his father in a foreign land, and even honoured the oath of the dying patriarch, shows again the favour God had given Joseph in the sight of Pharaoh (Genesis 41:40), a favour now overflowing to the whole covenant family.
7:
"And Joseph went up to bury his father: and with him went up all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house, and all the elders of the land of Egypt," The witnesses note the great company going up to bury Jacob. On "Joseph went up to bury his father," the grammatical voices note the journey. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph went up to bury his father: and with him went up all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house, and all the elders of the land of Egypt" — Joseph went up to Canaan to bury Jacob, accompanied by a great company: the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house, and the elders of Egypt; the great funeral procession; the honor done to Jacob (the dignitaries of Egypt attending).

On "all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house," the puritan Henry and witnesses note the honor. Henry and JFB note the great train (the court and the elders of Egypt) — the honor to Joseph and to Jacob; the dignity of the procession; the esteem of the patriarch.

On "all the elders of the land of Egypt," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the dignity: "all the elders of the land of Egypt" — the chief men of Egypt accompanying; the great honor; the providence (the covenant patriarch honored by the nation); the dignity of the burial (the faith that buried in Canaan, attended by the great). The verse displays the great company going up to bury Jacob.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the company — "Joseph went up to bury his father: and with him went up all the servants of Pharaoh... and all the elders of the land of Egypt," the great procession (Keil, Gill); "all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his house," the honor (Henry, JFB); "all the elders of the land of Egypt," the dignity (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying company. The verse marks the journey: to bury. The verse displays the servants: of Pharaoh. The verse displays the elders: of Egypt. The verse displays the honor: great. The company displays the great honor done to Jacob. The verse displays the Joseph: going up. The verse displays the procession: great. The verse displays the honor: of the nation. The great company going up to bury Jacob displays the honor of the patriarch: Joseph went up with all the servants of Pharaoh and the elders of Egypt — the great funeral procession, the covenant patriarch honored by the nation. The verse displays the journeying company, with their households (vv.8-9).
8:
"And all the house of Joseph, and his brethren, and his father's house: only their little ones, and their flocks, and their herds, they left in the land of Goshen." The very brief verse notes Jacob's family joining the procession, leaving the little ones in Goshen. The grammatical voices note the family. Gill notes that "all the house of Joseph, and his brethren, and his father's house: only their little ones, and their flocks, and their herds, they left in the land of Goshen" — the whole family of Jacob (Joseph's house, his brethren, his father's house) joined the procession to Canaan; only the little children and the flocks and herds were left in Goshen (as a pledge of return, and for the care of the young and the cattle); the family attending the burial.

On "all the house of Joseph, and his brethren, and his father's house," the witnesses note the family. Gill notes the whole family joining (the covenant household honoring the patriarch) — the family procession; the burial attended by all.

On "only their little ones... they left in the land of Goshen," the grammatical-historical note the pledge: "only their little ones, and their flocks, and their herds, they left in the land of Goshen" — the children and cattle left behind (a pledge of their return to Egypt, vv.14; and the care of the young); the providence (the family to return to Goshen until the Exodus). The verse displays Jacob's family joining the procession.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the family — "all the house of Joseph, and his brethren, and his father's house... only their little ones... they left in the land of Goshen," the family procession (Gill); "all the house of Joseph, and his brethren," the family (Gill); "only their little ones... they left in the land of Goshen," the pledge (v.14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the journeying family. The verse marks the family: all joined. The verse displays the little ones: left behind. The verse displays the Goshen: the pledge. The verse displays the burial: attended. The procession displays the family honoring the patriarch. The verse displays the family: joining. The verse displays the children: in Goshen. The verse displays the return: pledged. Jacob's family joining the procession, leaving the little ones in Goshen, displays the family honoring the patriarch: all the house joined, only the children and cattle left in Goshen (v.14) — the family attending the burial, the young left as a pledge of return. The verse displays the great procession (v.9).
9:
"And there went up with him both chariots and horsemen: and it was a very great company." The very brief verse notes the very great company with chariots and horsemen. The grammatical voices note the procession. Ellicott and Gill note that "there went up with him both chariots and horsemen: and it was a very great company" — the procession included chariots and horsemen (a military escort, for the long journey and the honor); the very great company (the magnitude of the funeral train); the honor done to Jacob; the dignity of the procession (befitting the father of the governor of Egypt).

On "both chariots and horsemen," the witnesses note the escort. Gill notes the chariots and horsemen (the escort, for safety on the journey, and for the state of the procession) — the dignity; the military honor.

On "it was a very great company," the grammatical-historical note the magnitude: "it was a very great company" — the great train (the court, the elders, the family, the escort); the magnitude of the honor; the providence (the covenant patriarch borne to Canaan with great pomp); the burial attended by the great. The verse displays the very great company.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the procession — "there went up with him both chariots and horsemen: and it was a very great company," the escort (Ellicott, Gill); "both chariots and horsemen," the military honor (Gill); "it was a very great company," the magnitude (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great company. The verse marks the chariots: and horsemen. The verse displays the escort: military. The verse displays the company: very great. The verse displays the honor: magnified. The procession displays the great honor done to Jacob. The verse displays the chariots: in the train. The verse displays the company: great. The verse displays the dignity: full. The very great company with chariots and horsemen displays the magnitude of the honor: there went chariots and horsemen, a very great company — the great funeral train, the patriarch borne to Canaan with pomp. The verse displays the mourning at Atad (vv.10-11).
10:
"And they came to the threshingfloor of Atad, which is beyond Jordan, and there they mourned with a great and very sore lamentation: and he made a mourning for his father seven days." The witnesses note the great mourning at the threshingfloor of Atad. On "they came to the threshingfloor of Atad, which is beyond Jordan," the grammatical voices note the place. Ellicott and Gill note that "they came to the threshingfloor of Atad, which is beyond Jordan, and there they mourned with a great and very sore lamentation" — the company came to Atad (a threshingfloor, beyond, i.e., on the way to, Jordan) and there held a great mourning; the solemn lamentation; the place renamed for the mourning (Abel-mizraim, v.11); the great grief.

On "they mourned with a great and very sore lamentation," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the lamentation. Clarke and Gill note the great mourning (the lamentation, the funeral rites, with the loud Eastern grief) — the solemn mourning for Jacob; the depth of the grief; the honor to the patriarch.

On "he made a mourning for his father seven days," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the mourning: "he made a mourning for his father seven days" — Joseph's seven days of mourning (the customary period, as for Saul, 1 Samuel 31:13); the formal lamentation; the honor to Jacob; the providence (the patriarch mourned with great honor on the way to the burial in Canaan). The verse displays the great mourning at Atad.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the place — "they came to the threshingfloor of Atad... and there they mourned," Atad (v.11, Ellicott, Gill); "they mourned with a great and very sore lamentation," the lamentation (Clarke, Gill); "he made a mourning for his father seven days," the mourning (1 Samuel 31:13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the mourning company. The verse marks the place: Atad. The verse displays the lamentation: great. The verse displays the mourning: seven days. The verse displays the honor: to Jacob. The mourning displays the great grief for the patriarch. The verse displays the company: mourning. The verse displays the Atad: the place. The verse displays the seven days: of mourning. The great mourning at the threshingfloor of Atad displays the honor and grief: they mourned with a great lamentation, seven days (1 Samuel 31:13) — the solemn mourning for Jacob on the way to the burial. The verse displays the renamed place (v.11).
11:
"And when the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, saw the mourning in the floor of Atad, they said, This is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians: wherefore the name of it was called Abelmizraim, which is beyond Jordan." The very brief verse notes the place named Abel-mizraim for the mourning. The grammatical voices note the naming. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, saw the mourning... they said, This is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians: wherefore the name of it was called Abelmizraim" — the Canaanites, seeing the great mourning (the Egyptian company lamenting), said it was a grievous mourning of the Egyptians; the place named Abel-mizraim ("the mourning of the Egyptians"); the memorial of the great lamentation; the renaming for the event.

On "This is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the observation. Wesley and Ellicott note the Canaanites' remark (the great mourning of the Egyptians, observed by the people of the land) — the impression made by the solemn lamentation; the witness of the grief.

On "the name of it was called Abelmizraim," the grammatical-historical note the memorial: "the name of it was called Abelmizraim" — the place named (Abel-mizraim, the mourning of the Egyptians); the memorial of the event (a play on Abel, "mourning," and "meadow"); the lasting name; the honor to Jacob remembered. The verse displays the place named Abel-mizraim.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the naming — "the inhabitants... saw the mourning... wherefore the name of it was called Abelmizraim," the renaming (Keil, Gill); "This is a grievous mourning to the Egyptians," the observation (Wesley, Ellicott); "the name of it was called Abelmizraim," the memorial (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named place. The verse marks the Canaanites: observing. The verse displays the mourning: grievous. The verse displays the name: Abel-mizraim. The verse displays the mourning: remembered. The place named Abel-mizraim for the mourning displays the memorial: the Canaanites called it Abel-mizraim, the mourning of the Egyptians — the lasting memorial of the great lamentation for Jacob. The verse displays the burial in Canaan (vv.12-13).
12:
"And his sons did unto him according as he commanded them:" The very brief verse notes Jacob's sons fulfilling his burial charge. The grammatical voices note the obedience. Gill and Clarke note that "his sons did unto him according as he commanded them" — Jacob's sons carried out his charge concerning his burial (49:29-32): to bury him in the cave of Machpelah in Canaan; the obedience of the sons; the fulfilling of the dying father's command; the honor to Jacob.

On "did unto him according as he commanded them," the witnesses note the obedience. Gill notes the sons' obedience to Jacob's charge (49:29-32) — the carrying out of the burial command; the dutiful sons; the honor to the father.

On the charge, the grammatical-historical note the burial: the sons fulfilling the charge (the burial in Machpelah, v.13) — the obedience to the dying father; the burial in the covenant land (the faith of the patriarchs, Hebrews 11:13-22); the providence (the patriarch laid with his fathers). The verse displays Jacob's sons fulfilling his burial charge.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the obedience — "his sons did unto him according as he commanded them," the fulfilling (49:29, Gill, Clarke); "did unto him according as he commanded them," the obedience (Gill); the burial (v.13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the obedient sons. The verse marks the charge: fulfilled. The verse displays the sons: obeying. The verse displays the burial: in Canaan. The verse displays the honor: to the father. The obedience displays the sons fulfilling the charge. The verse displays the sons: obeying. The verse displays the command: kept. The verse displays the burial: to come. Jacob's sons fulfilling his burial charge displays the obedience: his sons did as he commanded (49:29-32) — the carrying out of the dying father's charge, the burial in the covenant land. The verse displays the burial in Machpelah (v.13). That the sons who had once wronged their brother and grieved their father now carried out his dying charge to the letter shows the work of grace in them, the same changed hearts that had stood surety for Benjamin (Genesis 44:33) now rendering the last act of filial obedience to the patriarch.
13:
"For his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah, which Abraham bought with the field for a possession of a buryingplace of Ephron the Hittite, before Mamre." The witnesses note Jacob buried in the cave of Machpelah. On "his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah," the grammatical voices note the burial. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah" — Jacob's sons carried his body to Canaan and buried him in the cave of Machpelah (the family sepulchre); the burial in the covenant land; the patriarch laid with his fathers (Abraham, Isaac, Sarah, Rebekah, Leah, 49:31).

On "which Abraham bought with the field... of Ephron the Hittite," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the sepulchre. JFB and Gill note the cave of Machpelah (bought by Abraham, 23:16-20, for a burying-place) — the family tomb; the only possession the patriarchs held in the promised land (a pledge of the inheritance, Hebrews 11:13); the burial-place of the fathers.

On "before Mamre," the grammatical-historical note the place: "before Mamre" — the place (near Hebron, 23:19); the family sepulchre; the burial of Jacob with his fathers (the faith that buried in Canaan, looking for the promised inheritance, Hebrews 11:13-22); the patriarch laid in the covenant land. The verse displays Jacob buried in the cave of Machpelah.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the burial — "his sons carried him into the land of Canaan, and buried him in the cave of the field of Machpelah," the burial (49:31, Keil, Gill); "which Abraham bought... of Ephron the Hittite," the sepulchre (23:16, JFB, Gill); "before Mamre," the place (23:19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the buried Jacob. The verse marks the burial: in Machpelah. The verse displays the cave: Abraham's. The verse displays the Mamre: the place. The verse displays the covenant land: the burial. Jacob buried in the cave of Machpelah displays the burial in the covenant land: his sons buried him in Machpelah, which Abraham bought (23:16-20; 49:31) — the patriarch laid with his fathers in the promised land, a pledge of the inheritance. The verse displays the returning company (v.14).
14:
"And Joseph returned into Egypt, he, and his brethren, and all that went up with him to bury his father, after he had buried his father." The very brief verse notes the company returning to Egypt after the burial. The grammatical voices note the return. Gill and Calvin note that "Joseph returned into Egypt, he, and his brethren, and all that went up with him... after he had buried his father" — Joseph and the company returned to Egypt after burying Jacob; the keeping of the promise (to return, v.5); the going back to Goshen (where the little ones were, v.8); the resuming of the sojourn (until the Exodus).

On "Joseph returned into Egypt," the witnesses note the return. Gill notes Joseph's return (the promise kept, v.5) — the going back to Egypt; the resuming of his office; the sojourn continued.

On "after he had buried his father," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the close: "after he had buried his father" — the burial accomplished (the oath fulfilled, v.5); the return to Egypt; the providence (the family to remain in Egypt until the Exodus, the burial in Canaan a pledge of the future return). The verse displays the company returning to Egypt after the burial.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the return — "Joseph returned into Egypt... after he had buried his father," the return (v.5, Gill, Calvin); "Joseph returned into Egypt," the going back (Gill); "after he had buried his father," the close (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the returning Joseph. The verse marks the return: to Egypt. The verse displays the burial: accomplished. The verse displays the promise: kept. The verse displays the sojourn: resumed. The return displays the company going back to Egypt. The verse displays the Joseph: returning. The verse displays the burial: done. The verse displays the Egypt: resumed. The company returning to Egypt after the burial displays the promise kept: Joseph returned to Egypt after burying his father (v.5) — the oath fulfilled, the sojourn resumed until the Exodus. The verse displays the returned brethren, who fear Joseph (v.15).
15:
"And when Joseph's brethren saw that their father was dead, they said, Joseph will peradventure hate us, and will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him." The witnesses note the brethren's fear of Joseph's revenge. On "when Joseph's brethren saw that their father was dead," the grammatical voices note the fear. Henry and Gill note that "when Joseph's brethren saw that their father was dead, they said, Joseph will peradventure hate us, and will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him" — the brethren, now that Jacob was dead, feared that Joseph would now take revenge for the old wrong (the selling of him, 37:28); the guilty fear (the conscience still uneasy); the suspicion that Joseph's kindness had been only for the father's sake.

On "Joseph will peradventure hate us," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the suspicion. Calvin and Gill note the brethren's fear — that Joseph, the father gone, would hate and requite them (the guilty conscience, fearing revenge, though Joseph had freely forgiven them, 45:5-15); the lingering guilt; the misjudging of Joseph's heart.

On "will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the conscience: "will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him" — the brethren owning their evil (37:28) — the conscience still accusing; the fear of just requital; the occasion of Joseph's renewed forgiveness (vv.19-21); the guilt that the gospel of free forgiveness alone can quiet. The verse displays the brethren's fear of Joseph's revenge.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fear — "Joseph will peradventure hate us, and will certainly requite us all the evil," the guilty fear (37:28, Henry, Gill); "Joseph will peradventure hate us," the suspicion (45:5, Calvin, Gill); "will certainly requite us all the evil which we did unto him," the conscience (vv.19-21, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fearing brethren. The verse marks the father: dead. The verse displays the fear: of revenge. The verse displays the evil: owned. The verse displays the conscience: accusing. The fear displays the brethren's guilty suspicion. The verse displays the brethren: fearing. The verse displays the Joseph: misjudged. The verse displays the guilt: lingering. The brethren's fear of Joseph's revenge displays the guilty conscience: Joseph will hate us and requite the evil we did (37:28; 45:5) — the lingering guilt, fearing revenge though Joseph had forgiven them. The verse displays the message they send (vv.16-17).
16:
"And they sent a messenger unto Joseph, saying, Thy father did command before he died, saying," The very brief verse notes the brethren sending a message in Jacob's name. The grammatical voices note the message. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they sent a messenger unto Joseph, saying, Thy father did command before he died" — the brethren, fearing Joseph (v.15), sent a message (or came themselves, v.18) claiming that Jacob, before dying, had commanded Joseph to forgive them; the appeal to the father's authority; the seeking of forgiveness (whether Jacob truly so charged, or the brethren urged it as his likely wish, the witnesses differ).

On "they sent a messenger unto Joseph," the witnesses note the appeal. Gill notes the brethren's message (sent through a messenger, or themselves) — the seeking of reconciliation; the fear driving them to appeal; the message of entreaty.

On "Thy father did command before he died," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the plea: "Thy father did command before he died" — the appeal to Jacob's charge (that Joseph forgive them); whether Jacob actually so commanded (some hold he did, knowing the brethren's fear; others that the brethren reasonably inferred his wish, or even slightly stretched it in their fear) — the plea grounded on the father's authority; the seeking of forgiveness (v.17). The verse displays the brethren sending a message in Jacob's name.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the message — "they sent a messenger unto Joseph... Thy father did command before he died," the appeal (v.15, Keil, Gill); "they sent a messenger unto Joseph," the entreaty (Gill); "Thy father did command before he died," the plea (v.17, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appealing brethren. The verse marks the message: sent. The verse displays the father: his charge. The verse displays the forgiveness: sought. The verse displays the fear: driving them. The message displays the brethren's appeal for forgiveness. The verse displays the brethren: sending. The verse displays the father: invoked. The verse displays the plea: forming. The brethren sending a message in Jacob's name displays the appeal for forgiveness: thy father did command before he died (v.15) — the plea grounded on the father's authority, the seeking of reconciliation. The verse displays the message of forgiveness (v.17).
17:
"So shall ye say unto Joseph, Forgive, I pray thee now, the trespass of thy brethren, and their sin; for they did unto thee evil: and now, we pray thee, forgive the trespass of the servants of the God of thy father. And Joseph wept when they spake unto him." The witnesses note the brethren's plea for forgiveness and Joseph's tears. On "Forgive, I pray thee now, the trespass of thy brethren, and their sin," the grammatical voices note the plea. Clarke and Gill note that "Forgive, I pray thee now, the trespass of thy brethren, and their sin; for they did unto thee evil" — the brethren (in Jacob's name, and their own) pleaded for forgiveness of their trespass and sin (the selling of Joseph, 37:28); the open confession of their evil; the earnest entreaty for pardon.

On "forgive the trespass of the servants of the God of thy father," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the appeal. Wesley and Gill note the appeal — "the servants of the God of thy father" (the brethren pleading as fellow-worshippers of the covenant God, appealing to Joseph's piety, that he forgive as God's servant); the ground of the plea (the common faith); the earnest petition.

On "Joseph wept when they spake unto him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the tears: "Joseph wept when they spake unto him" — Joseph wept (grieved that his brethren still feared him, still doubted his forgiveness; the tears of wounded love, that they thought him capable of revenge after all his kindness, 45:5-15); the tenderness of Joseph (no thought of revenge, only grief at their distrust); the heart of true forgiveness (pained by their fear). The verse displays the brethren's plea and Joseph's tears.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plea — "Forgive, I pray thee now, the trespass of thy brethren... for they did unto thee evil," the confession (37:28, Clarke, Gill); "the servants of the God of thy father," the appeal (Wesley, Gill); "Joseph wept when they spake unto him," the tears (45:5, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the pleading brethren. The verse marks the plea: forgive. The verse displays the trespass: confessed. The verse displays the appeal: God's servants. The verse displays the tears: of Joseph. The plea displays the brethren seeking pardon and Joseph's grief. The verse displays the brethren: pleading. The verse displays the Joseph: weeping. The verse displays the forgiveness: sought. The brethren's plea for forgiveness and Joseph's tears display the seeking of pardon and the wounded love: forgive the trespass of thy brethren; and Joseph wept (37:28; 45:5) — the open confession, and Joseph's grief that they still feared him after all his kindness. The verse displays the prostrate brethren (v.18).
18:
"And his brethren also went and fell down before his face; and they said, Behold, we be thy servants." The very brief verse notes the brethren prostrating themselves before Joseph. The grammatical voices note the submission. Gill notes that "his brethren also went and fell down before his face; and they said, Behold, we be thy servants" — the brethren came themselves and fell down before Joseph, offering to be his servants (slaves); the deep submission (the guilty fear); the prostration (a further, full fulfillment of Joseph's dreams, 37:7-10, the sheaves and stars bowing); the offer of bondage (as Judah had offered, 44:33).

On "fell down before his face," the witnesses note the prostration. Gill notes the brethren falling down (the deep submission, the dreams fulfilled, 37:7-10) — the humbling before Joseph; the guilty fear driving them low.

On "Behold, we be thy servants," the grammatical-historical note the offer: "Behold, we be thy servants" — the brethren offering to be Joseph's slaves (the full submission); the guilty fear (offering bondage to escape revenge); the occasion of Joseph's gracious reply (vv.19-21); the dreams fulfilled (the brethren bowing). The verse displays the brethren prostrating themselves before Joseph.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the submission — "his brethren also went and fell down before his face... Behold, we be thy servants," the prostration (37:7, Gill); "fell down before his face," the submission (37:10, Gill); "Behold, we be thy servants," the offer (44:33; vv.19-21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prostrate brethren. The verse marks the falling: before Joseph. The verse displays the submission: deep. The verse displays the servants: offered. The verse displays the dreams: fulfilled. The prostration displays the brethren's full submission. The verse displays the brethren: prostrate. The verse displays the bondage: offered. The verse displays the dreams: fulfilled. The brethren prostrating themselves before Joseph displays the deep submission: they fell down, Behold, we be thy servants (37:7-10; 44:33) — the guilty fear offering bondage, the dreams fully fulfilled. The verse displays the reassuring Joseph (vv.19-21). That the brethren who had once stripped Joseph of his coat and sold him for silver (Genesis 37:28) now fell at his feet offering themselves as his slaves shows the full and final fulfilment of the dreams God had given the boy (Genesis 37:7-10), and sets the stage for the great word of forgiveness and providence that follows (Genesis 50:20).
19:
"And Joseph said unto them, Fear not: for am I in the place of God?" The witnesses note Joseph refusing the place of God — the crux of vengeance left to God. On "Fear not," the grammatical voices note the reassurance. Ellicott and Gill note that "Joseph said unto them, Fear not" — Joseph reassured his brethren (against their guilty fear, v.15); the gentle comfort; the free forgiveness (no thought of revenge); the calming of their fear.

On "for am I in the place of God?" interpreters expound the question. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Joseph's word — "am I in the place of God?": Joseph disclaimed the place of God — meaning either (a) "Am I to act as God, to take to myself the vengeance and judgment that belong to God alone?" (Joseph would not usurp God's prerogative of requital, Deuteronomy 32:35; Romans 12:19, "Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord"); or (b) "Am I in God's stead, to oppose what God has done?" — God having overruled their evil for good (v.20), Joseph would not resist God's purpose; the witnesses note both senses join: Joseph leaves vengeance to God (not usurping his place), and submits to God's overruling providence (which turned the evil to good); the meekness that forgives, leaving judgment to God; the godly refusal of revenge.

On the question, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the example: Joseph's "am I in the place of God?" — the model of forgiveness (leaving vengeance to God, Romans 12:19; the meek heart that requites not evil for evil, 1 Peter 3:9); the example of the godly (and a type of Christ, who forgave his murderers, Luke 23:34); the refusal to avenge. The verse displays Joseph refusing the place of God.

The witnesses agree the substance: Joseph bade them fear not, disclaiming the place of God (Ellicott, Calvin, Gill). The phrase bears its weight as the crux of vengeance left to God: "am I in the place of God?" — Joseph refusing to usurp the vengeance and judgment that belong to God alone (Deuteronomy 32:35; Romans 12:19), and submitting to God's overruling providence (which turned their evil to good, v.20); the meekness that forgives, leaving judgment to God, a type of Christ forgiving his murderers (Luke 23:34). The verse displays the place-of-God crux. The verse marks the reassurance: fear not. The verse displays the refusal: the place of God. The verse displays the vengeance: left to God. The verse displays the meekness: forgiving. The refusal displays Joseph leaving vengeance to God. The verse displays the Joseph: forgiving. The verse displays the God: the avenger. The verse displays the meekness: shown. Joseph refusing the place of God displays the crux: fear not; am I in the place of God? (Deuteronomy 32:35; Romans 12:19; Luke 23:34) — Joseph refusing to usurp God's vengeance, submitting to God's overruling providence, the meekness that forgives. The verse displays the forgiving Joseph, owning the providence (v.20).
20:
"But as for you, ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good, to bring to pass, as it is this day, to save much people alive." The witnesses note the great providence — evil meant for good — the crux of the whole Joseph story. On "ye thought evil against me," the grammatical voices note the brethren's sin. Ellicott and Gill note that "ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good" — Joseph acknowledged the brethren's real evil intent (the selling of him, 37:28, was truly their wicked deed, meant for harm); the honest naming of their sin (not excusing it); the real guilt owned.

On "but God meant it unto good," interpreters expound the providence. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great word — "but God meant it unto good": the same act that the brethren intended for evil, God intended (meant, purposed) for good; the providence of God overruling the evil of men for good — not that God did the evil (the brethren's sin was truly theirs, freely and wickedly done), nor that the evil was good, but that God, in his secret, sovereign providence, directed and overruled their evil to accomplish his good purpose (the preservation of the family and the nations); the harmonizing of human responsibility and divine sovereignty (man's wicked intent and God's good purpose in the one act, as supremely in the cross, Acts 2:23, "him, being delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God, ye have... slain"; 4:27-28); the deep truth of providence (the great lesson of the whole Joseph history, and indeed of Genesis) — God bringing good out of evil, ruling the sins of men to his own ends without being the author of them; the comfort to the afflicted (God overruling all for good, Romans 8:28).

On "to save much people alive," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the purpose: "to bring to pass, as it is this day, to save much people alive" — God's good purpose: the saving of many (Egypt, the nations, and Jacob's house) from the famine (45:5-7); the preservation of the covenant family (from which the Messiah would come); the providence that turned the brethren's crime into the means of salvation; the good God brought out of the evil. The verse displays the great providence — evil meant for good.

The witnesses agree the substance: the brethren meant evil, but God meant it unto good, to save much people alive (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the whole story: "ye thought evil against me; but God meant it unto good" — the same act the brethren intended for harm (their real sin, 37:28), God sovereignly purposed and overruled for good (not the author of the evil, yet ruling it to his ends), the harmonizing of human guilt and divine sovereignty (as in the cross, Acts 2:23; 4:27-28), God bringing good out of evil to save much people alive (45:5-7; Romans 8:28). The verse displays the providence crux. The verse marks the evil: man's intent. The verse displays the good: God's purpose. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The verse displays the salvation: of many. The providence displays God ruling evil for good. The verse displays the men: meaning evil. The verse displays the God: meaning good. The verse displays the salvation: wrought. The great providence — evil meant for good — displays the crux: ye thought evil, but God meant it unto good, to save much people alive (37:28; 45:5-7; Acts 2:23; Romans 8:28) — the brethren's real sin sovereignly overruled by God for good, the great lesson of providence. The verse displays the comforting Joseph (v.21).
21:
"Now therefore fear ye not: I will nourish you, and your little ones. And he comforted them, and spake kindly unto them." The witnesses note Joseph comforting his brethren and promising to provide. On "fear ye not: I will nourish you, and your little ones," the grammatical voices note the comfort. Ellicott and Gill note that "fear ye not: I will nourish you, and your little ones" — Joseph, having owned the providence (v.20), reassured his brethren: he would not avenge, but nourish (feed, provide for) them and their children; the free forgiveness sealed with kindness; the promise of provision (as in the famine, 45:11); the comfort.

On "And he comforted them," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the comfort. Calvin and Gill note that Joseph "comforted them" (calmed their fears, reassured their guilty hearts) — the gentle comfort; the free forgiveness; the heart that requited evil with good (Romans 12:21).

On "and spake kindly unto them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the kindness: "spake kindly unto them" (Hebrew, "spake to their heart") — Joseph's tender, kind words (reassuring, affectionate); the heart-comfort; the model of forgiveness (the wronged comforting the wrongdoers, overcoming evil with good); the type of Christ comforting his erring people. The verse displays Joseph comforting his brethren and promising to provide.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the comfort — "fear ye not: I will nourish you... And he comforted them, and spake kindly unto them," the comfort and provision (45:11, Ellicott, Gill); "And he comforted them," the reassurance (Romans 12:21, Calvin, Gill); "spake kindly unto them," the tender words (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the comforting Joseph. The verse marks the comfort: fear not. The verse displays the provision: I will nourish. The verse displays the kindness: spoken to the heart. The verse displays the forgiveness: sealed. The comfort displays Joseph forgiving and providing. The verse displays the Joseph: comforting. The verse displays the brethren: reassured. The verse displays the kindness: tender. Joseph comforting his brethren and promising to provide displays the free forgiveness: fear not, I will nourish you; and he spake kindly unto them (45:11; Romans 12:21) — the wronged comforting the wrongdoers, overcoming evil with good. The verse displays the forgiving Joseph, who lives on in Egypt (v.22).
22:
"And Joseph dwelt in Egypt, he, and his father's house: and Joseph lived an hundred and ten years." The witnesses note Joseph's long life in Egypt. On "Joseph dwelt in Egypt, he, and his father's house," the grammatical voices note the dwelling. Henry and Gill note that "Joseph dwelt in Egypt, he, and his father's house: and Joseph lived an hundred and ten years" — Joseph and the family of Israel dwelt in Egypt (the sojourn continuing); Joseph living to a hundred and ten years (the full age, esteemed by the Egyptians the ideal life-span); the long life of the godly ruler; the close of Joseph's days approaching.

On "Joseph lived an hundred and ten years," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the age. Calvin and Clarke note Joseph's age (a hundred and ten, the Egyptians' ideal age) — the full life; the providence prolonging Joseph's days (to govern, and to see his seed, v.23); the long life of the faithful.

On the dwelling, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the sojourn: Joseph dwelling in Egypt with the family — the sojourn (the covenant family in Egypt until the Exodus); the providence (the seventy grown into a people, Exodus 1:7); the long life of Joseph closing the patriarchal age. The verse displays Joseph's long life in Egypt.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the life — "Joseph dwelt in Egypt... and Joseph lived an hundred and ten years," the long life (Henry, Gill); "Joseph lived an hundred and ten years," the age (Calvin, Clarke); the sojourn (Exodus 1:7, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the long-lived Joseph. The verse marks the dwelling: in Egypt. The verse displays the life: a hundred and ten. The verse displays the family: with him. The verse displays the sojourn: continuing. The life displays Joseph's full age in Egypt. The verse displays the Joseph: dwelling. The verse displays the age: full. The verse displays the sojourn: ongoing. Joseph's long life in Egypt displays the full age of the faithful: Joseph dwelt in Egypt and lived a hundred and ten years (Exodus 1:7) — the long life of the godly ruler, the sojourn continuing until the Exodus. The verse displays the aged Joseph, who sees his seed (v.23).
23:
"And Joseph saw Ephraim's children of the third generation: the children also of Machir the son of Manasseh were brought up upon Joseph's knees." The very brief verse notes Joseph seeing his children's children. The grammatical voices note the seed. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph saw Ephraim's children of the third generation: the children also of Machir the son of Manasseh were brought up upon Joseph's knees" — Joseph lived to see his descendants to the third (or fourth) generation (Ephraim's great-grandchildren, and Machir's children, of Manasseh); the blessing of long life and a numerous seed; the children "brought up upon Joseph's knees" (adopted, or fondled, as his own); the blessing of seeing his seed (Psalm 128:6).

On "Ephraim's children of the third generation," the witnesses note the seed. Gill notes Joseph seeing his descendants to the third generation (the blessing of a numerous posterity, the fulfillment of the blessing on Ephraim, 48:19) — the seed multiplied; the long life rewarded.

On "brought up upon Joseph's knees," the grammatical-historical note the blessing: "the children also of Machir... were brought up upon Joseph's knees" — Machir's children (of Manasseh) brought up on Joseph's knees (the token of adoption or fond care); the blessing of the godly old age (seeing the seed, Psalm 128:6); the providence (the seed growing toward the nation, Exodus 1:7). The verse displays Joseph seeing his children's children.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the seed — "Joseph saw Ephraim's children of the third generation... brought up upon Joseph's knees," the descendants (48:19, Keil, Gill); "Ephraim's children of the third generation," the seed (Gill); "brought up upon Joseph's knees," the blessing (Psalm 128:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed Joseph. The verse marks the seed: third generation. The verse displays the children: on his knees. The verse displays the blessing: long life. The verse displays the seed: multiplied. The seed displays Joseph's blessing of a numerous posterity. The verse displays the Joseph: seeing. The verse displays the seed: growing. The verse displays the blessing: of old age. Joseph seeing his children's children displays the blessing of the godly old age: Joseph saw Ephraim's third generation, and Machir's children on his knees (48:19; Psalm 128:6) — the blessing of long life and a numerous seed, growing toward the nation. The verse displays the dying Joseph, who foretells the visitation (v.24).
24:
"And Joseph said unto his brethren, I die: and God will surely visit you, and bring you out of this land unto the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob." The witnesses note Joseph's dying faith in the visitation and the return — the crux of the dying hope. On "I die: and God will surely visit you," the grammatical voices note the visitation. Ellicott and Gill note that "Joseph said unto his brethren, I die: and God will surely visit you, and bring you out of this land" — Joseph, dying, assured his brethren that God would surely "visit" them (come to them in mercy, to deliver) and bring them out of Egypt; the dying prophecy; the faith in the Exodus (foretold, 15:13-16); the certainty of God's deliverance.

On "and bring you out of this land," interpreters note the Exodus and the faith. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Joseph's dying faith — "bring you out of this land unto the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob": Joseph, by faith, foresaw and foretold the Exodus (the bringing out of Egypt to Canaan, the land of the covenant oath, 15:18; 26:3; 28:13); the faith that looked beyond Egypt to the promised land (Egypt not their home); the dying testimony to the covenant promise; the faith specially commended — "By faith Joseph, when he died, made mention of the departing of the children of Israel; and gave commandment concerning his bones" (Hebrews 11:22); the patriarch's hope handed on; the very promise on which the next book (Exodus) opens.

On "the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the covenant: "the land which he sware to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob" — the covenant land (the oath to the fathers, 15:18; 17:8; 26:3; 28:13); the inheritance promised; the faith that held the oath sure (though Egypt was their present home); the dying hope in the covenant (the bones to be carried up, v.25). The verse displays Joseph's dying faith in the visitation and the return.

The witnesses agree the substance: Joseph, dying, foretold by faith that God would visit them and bring them to the promised land (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the dying hope: "God will surely visit you, and bring you out of this land" — Joseph's faith foreseeing the Exodus (15:13-16) and the return to the covenant land (the oath to the fathers, 15:18; 28:13); the faith specially commended (Hebrews 11:22), looking beyond Egypt to the promised inheritance; the patriarch's hope on which Exodus would open. The verse displays the dying-hope crux. The verse marks the death: I die. The verse displays the visitation: God will surely. The verse displays the return: out of Egypt. The verse displays the faith: in the covenant. The faith displays Joseph's dying hope in the Exodus. The verse displays the Joseph: dying. The verse displays the visitation: foretold. The verse displays the land: promised. Joseph's dying faith in the visitation and the return displays the crux: God will surely visit you and bring you out unto the land he sware to the fathers (15:13-18; 28:13; Hebrews 11:22) — the dying faith foreseeing the Exodus, looking beyond Egypt to the promised inheritance. The verse displays the dying Joseph, charging concerning his bones (v.25).
25:
"And Joseph took an oath of the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you, and ye shall carry up my bones from hence." The witnesses note Joseph's oath concerning his bones. On "Joseph took an oath of the children of Israel," the grammatical voices note the oath. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph took an oath of the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you, and ye shall carry up my bones from hence" — Joseph made the children of Israel swear that, when God visited them and brought them out (the Exodus), they would carry his bones up out of Egypt (to be buried in Canaan); the dying charge; the faith in the return (the bones a pledge of the promised inheritance).

On "God will surely visit you," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the faith. JFB and Calvin note the repeated assurance ("God will surely visit you") — Joseph's certain faith in the Exodus (15:13-16); the ground of the oath (the sure deliverance); the dying confidence.

On "ye shall carry up my bones from hence," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the pledge: "ye shall carry up my bones from hence" — Joseph's charge that his bones be carried to Canaan (fulfilled, the bones carried at the Exodus, Exodus 13:19, and buried at Shechem, Joshua 24:32); the faith that would not rest in Egypt (the embalmed body a standing witness, awaiting the Exodus); the pledge of the promised land (the dying patriarch's hope, Hebrews 11:22; the bones a perpetual testimony to Israel that Egypt was not their home). The verse displays Joseph's oath concerning his bones.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the oath — "Joseph took an oath... ye shall carry up my bones from hence," the charge (Exodus 13:19, Keil, Gill); "God will surely visit you," the faith (15:13, JFB, Calvin); "ye shall carry up my bones from hence," the pledge (Joshua 24:32; Hebrews 11:22, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charging Joseph. The verse marks the oath: of the children. The verse displays the visitation: sure. The verse displays the bones: carried up. The verse displays the faith: in the return. The oath displays Joseph's faith pledging the return. The verse displays the Joseph: charging. The verse displays the bones: a pledge. The verse displays the Exodus: awaited. Joseph's oath concerning his bones displays the dying faith: ye shall carry up my bones, for God will surely visit you (Exodus 13:19; Joshua 24:32; Hebrews 11:22) — the bones a standing pledge of the promised inheritance, awaiting the Exodus. The verse displays the dying Joseph, coffined in Egypt (v.26).
26:
"So Joseph died, being an hundred and ten years old: and they embalmed him, and he was put in a coffin in Egypt." The witnesses note the death of Joseph, the close of Genesis — the crux of the unburied coffin. On "Joseph died, being an hundred and ten years old," the grammatical voices note the death. Ellicott and Gill note that "Joseph died, being an hundred and ten years old: and they embalmed him" — Joseph died at a hundred and ten (the full age, v.22), and was embalmed (the Egyptian custom, as Jacob, v.2); the death of the godly ruler; the close of the patriarchal age; the end of the generation that went down to Egypt.

On "they embalmed him," the witnesses note the preserving. Gill notes the embalming (as Jacob, v.2) — the body preserved (for the future carrying up, v.25); the Egyptian honor; the body kept against the Exodus.

On "he was put in a coffin in Egypt," interpreters expound the close. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading note the striking ending — "he was put in a coffin in Egypt": Joseph's embalmed body laid in a coffin (an ark, or chest) in Egypt — not buried in Canaan (like Jacob), but kept, the coffin a standing pledge and witness; the book of Genesis (which opened with creation and the garden) closes with a coffin in Egypt — the reign of death (the wages of sin, Romans 6:23) over even the best; yet the coffin "in Egypt" was not the end, but a pledge of the Exodus (the bones to be carried up, v.25; Exodus 13:19; Joshua 24:32); the faith that looked beyond the coffin to the promised land (and beyond death to the resurrection); the unburied coffin a perpetual sermon to Israel — that Egypt was not their home, that God would surely visit them; the hope that bridges Genesis to Exodus (the bondage and the deliverance to come). The verse displays the death of Joseph, the close of Genesis.

The witnesses agree the substance: Joseph died, was embalmed, and laid in a coffin in Egypt (Ellicott, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the unburied coffin: Genesis closing not with the promised land but with "a coffin in Egypt" — the reign of death over even the godly (Romans 6:23), yet the coffin a standing pledge of the Exodus (the bones to be carried up, v.25; Exodus 13:19; Joshua 24:32), the faith looking beyond the coffin to the promised land and the resurrection; the unburied coffin a perpetual witness that Egypt was not Israel's home, the hope bridging Genesis to Exodus. The verse displays the unburied-coffin crux. The verse marks the death: Joseph. The verse displays the coffin: in Egypt. The verse displays the pledge: of the Exodus. The verse displays the hope: beyond death. The death displays the close of Genesis in a coffin, yet in hope. The verse displays the Joseph: dead. The verse displays the coffin: a pledge. The verse displays the Exodus: awaited. The death of Joseph, the close of Genesis, displays the crux: Joseph died, and was put in a coffin in Egypt (v.25; Exodus 13:19; Hebrews 11:22) — the reign of death, yet the coffin a standing pledge of the Exodus, the faith looking beyond to the promised land. The verse closes the book of Genesis, opening on the hope of Exodus.
